skill.name: agent-memory-systems
skill.description: Memory is the cornerstone of intelligent agents. Without it, every interaction starts from zero. This skill covers the architecture of agent memory: short-term (context window), long-term (vector stores), episodic, semantic, and procedural memory types.
skill.summary: Design and implement memory systems for AI agents including retrieval strategies, chunking, and storage architecture.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill is a pure documentation resource about agent memory architecture. Static analysis flagged apparent security patterns (backtick syntax, cryptographic keywords), but manual review confirms these are false positives. The backticks are markdown code formatting in a skill list, not shell execution. No actual code, scripts, network calls, or cryptographic operations exist. This is safe for publication.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: External Commands Flag
medium_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' at SKILL.md:68. This is markdown code formatting for a list of related skills: `autonomous-agents`, `multi-agent-orchestration`, etc. No shell execution occurs.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' at SKILL.md:3 and SKILL.md:71. These are YAML frontmatter keywords and regular text about 'execution' - no cryptographic code exists.

content.value_statement: Build intelligent memory architectures for AI agents that enable long-term learning and context-aware responses. This skill provides patterns for implementing short-term, long-term, and episodic memory that improves agent performance over time.
actual_capabilities: Design memory architecture for AI agents using short-term, long-term, episodic, semantic, and procedural memory types
Select appropriate vector stores and chunking strategies for different retrieval needs
Implement memory retrieval systems that surface relevant context at the right time
Identify and avoid common memory system anti-patterns like storing everything forever
Apply contextual chunking techniques for improved retrieval quality
Balance token budgets across different memory types for optimal performance
limitations: This is a design pattern skill - it does not include executable code or implementations
Does not provide vector database setup or configuration guidance
Does not cover specific embedding model selection in depth
No runtime memory management code is included

use_cases.title: AI Application Developer Building Conversational Agents
use_cases.description: Design memory systems that allow chatbots to remember user preferences and conversation history across sessions, enabling more personalized and context-aware interactions.
use_cases.target_user: AI application developers creating conversational agents

use_cases.title: DevOps Engineer Building Autonomous Agents
use_cases.description: Implement long-term memory for agents that automate infrastructure tasks, allowing them to learn from previous deployments and avoid repeating mistakes.
use_cases.target_user: DevOps engineers building autonomous CI/CD agents

use_cases.title: Research Engineer Prototyping Agent Architectures
use_cases.description: Explore different memory system designs and retrieval strategies to find optimal configurations for specific agent use cases.
use_cases.target_user: Research engineers prototyping new agent architectures

prompt_templates.title: Design Memory Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a memory system for an AI agent that handles customer support conversations. The agent needs to remember user preferences, past issues, and resolution history. Specify the memory types, storage approach, and retrieval strategy.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building a customer support agent with persistent memory

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate Chunking Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this document chunking strategy for a knowledge base: [describe strategy]. What are the strengths and weaknesses? How would you improve retrieval accuracy?

prompt_templates.title: Fix Memory Anti-Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Our agent memory system is storing every conversation indefinitely. This is causing slow retrieval and high storage costs. What memory management approach should we use instead?

prompt_templates.title: Select Vector Store
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare vector database options for a production agent system handling 10 million embeddings with sub-100ms retrieval requirements. Consider Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, and pgvector.

output_examples.input: Design memory for a code review agent that learns from past reviews
output_examples.output: A code review agent should use episodic memory to store individual review sessions, semantic memory for learned coding patterns, and procedural memory for review workflows. Implement retrieval with temporal weighting to prioritize recent relevant reviews while avoiding stale context.

output_examples.input: What memory type should store user preferences?
output_examples.output: User preferences should be stored in semantic memory since they represent persistent learned knowledge about the user. Use episodic memory to track how preferences evolved over time. Apply metadata filtering to retrieve user-specific memories efficiently.

best_practices: Always filter by metadata (time, user, topic) before semantic retrieval to reduce noise and improve relevance
Implement memory decay or importance scoring to prevent old or irrelevant memories from polluting context
Budget tokens strategically across memory types - prioritize recent, high-relevance memories in limited context windows
anti_patterns: Storing everything forever without eviction policies - leads to retrieval pollution and increased costs
Using a single memory type for all data types - different information needs different storage approaches
Chunking documents without testing retrieval quality - chunks that look good semantically may not retrieve well

faq.question: What is the difference between short-term and long-term memory?
faq.answer: Short-term memory (working memory) lives in the context window and is available during a single conversation. Long-term memory persists across sessions and requires a vector store or database for retrieval.

faq.question: When should I use episodic vs semantic memory?
faq.answer: Use episodic memory for specific events or interactions (a conversation, a task completion). Use semantic memory for learned facts and patterns (user preferences, general knowledge).

faq.question: How do I prevent memory retrieval from returning irrelevant results?
faq.answer: Apply metadata filtering before semantic search (filter by time, user, topic). Use contextual chunking that preserves surrounding context. Implement importance scoring to boost recent or critical memories.

faq.question: What chunk size should I use for document storage?
faq.answer: There is no universal answer. Test different sizes with your actual retrieval queries. Smaller chunks (256-512 tokens) work well for precise matching, larger chunks (1024+) preserve more context but may introduce noise.

faq.question: Can Claude Code execute memory system code?
faq.answer: Claude Code can help design and explain memory systems but cannot execute them directly. This skill provides architectural guidance that developers implement in their own code.

faq.question: How do I handle conflicting memories?
faq.answer: Detect conflicts on storage by comparing new memories against existing ones. Store conflict markers or use versioning. At retrieval time, surface the most recent or highest-confidence memory and flag conflicts to the user.
